title: Deep Disagreement as a Challenge for Democratic Education: Learning to Compromise
author: Albrecht

opening_move.description: Democratic education scholarship calls for rethinking in response to deep disagreement, yet consistently returns to the same liberal and deliberative standard responses (Rawls, Habermas) that cannot address the problem. The puzzle: why do reassessments fail to move beyond the frameworks they critique?
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Deep disagreement poses an existential challenge to democratic education; without a better response, future citizens will be unprepared for the conflict-intensification characterizing contemporary democracies.

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper identifies three existing strategies in democratic education and performs immanent critique of each: (1) Rawlsian liberalism assumes common ground it cannot secure; (2) deliberative theory is too optimistic about reason's ability to find common ground; (3) liberal values cultivation externalizes the problem. The paper proposes compromise as a fourth approach that acknowledges persistent disagreement without presupposing common ground.

epistemic_mode.notes: Entirely conceptual. No empirical material. The argument proceeds by mapping three existing positions, performing immanent critique of each, and proposing compromise as the positive alternative.

normative_stake.domain: democratic education / conflict regulation

confidence_notes: Clearly Type 2 (polemical reconstruction): three-strategy mapping followed by serial immanent critique followed by positive proposal. The conclusion is open rather than strong because the compromise proposal is programmatic — what compromise-education would look like is gestured at rather than specified. This is close to TCS Type 2 (fully_normative, dialectical synthesis) but the paper is about philosophy of education rather than social theory proper.

title: The Hegemonic World Picture: Representation, Post-Truth, and Artificial Intelligence
author: Anastasiou

opening_move.description: Conventional critiques of post-truth politics adopt quasi-objectivist frameworks premised on restoring fact/representation correspondence — but this presupposes an essentialist account of truth that poststructuralist and postmodern thought has decisively undermined. The puzzle: how can a critique of post-truth be effective if it appeals to a discredited standard?
opening_move.stakes_claimed: An effective counter-offensive to post-truth politics requires working through the logic of representation rather than against it — leveraging representational ambiguity in the service of democracy.

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper constructs an alternative through a genealogy of representation from Plato through poststructuralism (Laclau-Mouffe, Derrida), then extends Laclauian hegemony theory to AI-powered digital communication. 'Total representations' (sloganesque, ambiguous, heuristic) are diagnosed as the medium of post-truth hegemony. The prescription: democratic politics should use rhetoric strategically, not try to restore truth.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Conceptual genealogy of 'representation' from classical metaphysics through constructivism to poststructuralism; Laclauian hegemony analysis applied to AI-mediated communication.
epistemic_mode.notes: No original empirical research. The argument is fully conceptual-normative: genealogy of representation + immanent critique of objectivist post-truth critique + hegemony framework extension to AI. Contemporary examples (social media, AI systems) are used as illustrative rather than evidentiary.

normative_stake.domain: democratic theory / epistemology / AI and public sphere

confidence_notes: Type 2 (polemical reconstruction / fully normative). The genealogical reconstruction of representation theory is central to the argument — it is the ground for the immanent critique of objectivism. The Laclauian hegemony framework is extended to AI-mediated communication to generate the 'total representations' concept. No case in the Type 3 sense — contemporary AI systems are backdrop, not an interpretive object. The conclusion is open because the prescriptive turn (use rhetoric for democracy) is gestured at rather than specified.

title: The Hegel Test
author: Bartonek

opening_move.description: Existing tests for machine intelligence (Turing, Coffee, Employment tests) set their criteria too low because they lack a systematic concept of thinking and human intelligence. Without a genuine high-point criterion, we cannot determine whether machines have reached human-level intelligence.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: A proper test of strong AI requires Hegel's philosophy of negativity, recognition, and labour — the human capacity for self-distancing, criticality, and dialectical self-consciousness — as the benchmark.

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper performs immanent critique of existing AI intelligence tests, arguing each lacks a systematic concept of thinking. Hegel's negativity, recognition, and labour dialectics are proposed as the constitutive elements of human cognition that a true intelligence test must measure. The 'Hegel test' requires machines to demonstrate mastery of negativity (self-critical distance), recognition (dialectics of master and slave), and transformative labour.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Close reading of Hegel's Phenomenology of Spirit and Science of Logic (sections on negativity, master-slave dialectics, labour); critical analysis of AI intelligence frameworks (Bostrom, Kurzweil, Chollet, Legg-Hutter).
epistemic_mode.notes: The philosophical text (Hegel) is used as a resource for constructing the test, not as an interpretive case in the Type 3 sense. The argument is fully normative-conceptual: critique of existing tests + Hegelian framework construction + specification of the Hegel test components.

normative_stake.domain: philosophy of mind / AI / critical theory

confidence_notes: Type 2 (dialectical synthesis variant): serial immanent critique of four existing AI tests, followed by Hegelian framework construction as positive alternative. The conclusion is strong because the paper fully specifies the Hegel test's three components and declares them constitutive of genuine intelligence. Unusual topical range for PSC (AI/philosophy of mind) but pure Type 2 argument structure.

title: Expropriation of the Expropriators
author: Blumenfeld

opening_move.description: Marx's phrase 'expropriation of the expropriators' is famous but rarely analysed: Marxists read it either as objective historical tendency (determinism) or as revolutionary political praxis (voluntarism), without resolving the tension. The paper argues it does both — and that resolving this tension is philosophically and politically essential.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Understanding expropriation correctly allows us to ground contemporary struggles (housing, climate, work) in Marx's immanent logic of capitalist self-expropriation rather than in external moral demands.

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper synthesizes the two Marxist readings by grounding revolutionary praxis within the objective dynamics of capital's self-expropriation. Drawing on close reading of Marx's texts (Capital, Civil War in France, Grundrisse), it argues that the active (subjective) and passive (objective) meanings of expropriation are mediated and mutually dependent.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Close textual reading of Marx (Capital Vol. I, Civil War in France, Grundrisse, Economic Manuscripts); analysis of legal frameworks of expropriation (illustrative, not constitutive).
epistemic_mode.notes: The legal expropriation section is illustrative background — real content is immanent reading of Marx's texts. The contemporary applications (housing, climate, work) in the conclusion are programmatic normative derivations, not empirical analysis. Classified fully_normative: the argument moves entirely within Marx's conceptual framework.

normative_stake.domain: Marxist political theory / property / revolution

confidence_notes: Type 2b (genealogical deepening variant): close reading of Marx as genealogical reconstruction of the concept, followed by synthesis of determinism/voluntarism antinomy, strong prescriptive conclusion. The philosophical text (Marx) is used as resource not as interpretive case — the paper's argument is about what Marx means and how to apply it, not about reading Marx as a cultural object that generates the argument.

title: Freedom of Speech: A Relational Defence
author: Bonotti and Seglow

opening_move.description: The literature on free speech focuses on limits and regulation but has not advanced its normative foundations significantly in decades. Three classic approaches (truth, autonomy, democracy) each fail to satisfy four intuitive desiderata for an adequate theory of free speech.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: A new relational view — grounding free speech's value in the recognition speakers accord each other, and its limits in republican non-domination — better satisfies all four desiderata and has productive implications for hate speech and fake news.

theoretical_positioning.description: Each classic theory is evaluated against four desiderata (specialness, stringency, no internal balancing, opportunity). Each fails on at least two counts. The relational view synthesizes recognition theory (Hegel/Honneth tradition) with republican liberty (non-dominated co-exercisable) to meet all four, then illustrates implications for hate speech and fake news.

epistemic_mode.notes: Analytic political philosophy. Argument proceeds through desiderata-construction, critical evaluation of existing theories, and positive construction of the relational view. Hate speech and fake news are used as illustrative applications, not empirical cases.

normative_stake.domain: political philosophy / freedom of speech / liberal theory

confidence_notes: Classic Type 2a (dialectical synthesis): three rivals evaluated and found wanting, new framework proposed that satisfies the desiderata. Analytic political philosophy style closer to standard phil. journals, but within PSC's normative-critical range. Strong conclusion because the relational view is fully specified and applied. Note: this paper differs from the TCS corpus papers in using formal desiderata and analytic argument structure rather than Continental critical theory idiom — a PSC-specific variant.

title: The Public Sphere and Democracy in Transformation: Continuing the Debate – An Introduction
author: Brunkhorst, Seeliger, Sevignani

opening_move.description: All dimensions of the current 'tragedy of democracy' — technocracy, populism, environmental devastation, material asymmetry — share a common reference point: the public sphere. This special issue introduction argues for that thesis and maps how contributors address the nexus of democracy and public sphere.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: The public sphere is the central site for understanding democratic decline and the central requirement for democratic recovery.

theoretical_positioning.description: The introduction maps four dimensions of 'the public' (res publica, political authority benchmark, popular will expression, communicative space) via Rinken's taxonomy, then argues all current democratic pathologies converge on public sphere diagnosis. It orients the special issue rather than advancing a thesis of its own.

epistemic_mode.notes: Special-issue introduction. Maps the intellectual landscape, identifies the central concept (public sphere), and orients contributors. No original argument beyond the integrating thesis.

normative_stake.domain: democratic theory / public sphere

confidence_notes: Type 1 (special-issue introduction): orients the special issue, maps the public sphere concept, identifies the unifying thesis (public sphere as common reference point of democratic crisis), research_agenda closure. Identical genre and structure to seeliger-sevignani_2022 in the TCS corpus.

title: Racism, Epistemic Injustice, and Ideology Critique
author: Bufkin

opening_move.description: Fricker's influential epistemic injustice framework has generated extensive applications, but its individualist, cognitivist, and dematerialized account of racialized epistemic dysfunction is inadequate to explain racialized imaginaries, structural racism, and the social hegemony of racist ideas. The puzzle: why does the most sophisticated recent framework for understanding racist knowledge still fall short?
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Returning to a materialist theory of ideology (Stuart Hall's Marxist-derived approach) better accounts for how racist ideas become socially hegemonic and why individualist epistemic remediation fails to undo racialized worlds.

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper performs a sustained immanent critique of Fricker: (1) testimonial injustice level — overly rationalist, disembodied model of the knower misses psychosocial dynamics; (2) structural level — narrow propositional account of hermeneutical resources misses racialized imaginaries that are socially hegemonic despite being epistemically suspect. The alternative is Stuart Hall's materialist ideology theory: ideologies emerge from distortions of concepts internal to social practices, not from individual epistemic failings.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Close reading of Fricker's Epistemic Injustice; critical engagement with Hall's cultural Marxism and theory of ideology; conceptual analysis of testimonial and hermeneutical injustice.
epistemic_mode.notes: Fully normative: argument moves within the conceptual space of social epistemology and ideology theory. Fricker's framework is the primary target; Hall's work is the positive resource. No empirical material — examples of institutional racism (policing, housing market) are illustrative reference points, not analysed cases.

normative_stake.domain: social epistemology / racism / ideology critique

confidence_notes: Type 2a (dialectical synthesis): single target (Fricker) subjected to thorough immanent critique at two levels, positive alternative (Hall) proposed. Strong conclusion. The immanent critique is unusually focused on a single book rather than a tradition, making this a precision variant of Type 2.

title: The Appropriating Subject: Cultural Appreciation, Property and Entitlement
author: Cattien and Stopford

opening_move.description: The standard distinction between cultural appropriation (taking) and cultural appreciation (not taking) is used to defuse concerns about cultural theft: appreciation is presented as unproblematic because it involves no taking. This paper diagnoses this as a false distinction resting on a false normative principle.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Appropriation is already built into appreciation: the hegemonic subject manifests the cultural object in advance, constituting it as available for aesthetic consumption. This ontological critique undermines the exculpatory function of the appreciation/appropriation distinction.

rivals_engaged: standard analytic aesthetics account of appreciation (Gracyk, Levinson, Iseminger)
theoretical_positioning.description: The paper argues that the standard aesthetic account of 'appreciation' (responding to what is already there) presupposes a false metaphysics: what is 'already there' for the hegemonic appreciating subject is itself constituted by relations of cultural power. Drawing on Hegel and post-colonial ontology, the paper proposes a 'critical ontology of hegemonic cultural appreciation' where manifestation-in-advance is itself an act of appropriation.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Conceptual analysis; ontological argumentation drawing on Hegel (phenomenology of recognition), Fanon, and social ontology of cultural power.
epistemic_mode.notes: The opening examples (Led Zeppelin, dreadlocks, Alan Watts) are illustrative philosophical thought experiments, not empirical cases. The argument is fully normative-ontological.

normative_stake.domain: aesthetics / cultural politics / postcolonial theory

confidence_notes: Type 2a (dialectical synthesis): immanent critique of standard aesthetics account of appreciation, followed by post-colonial ontological framework that collapses the appropriation/appreciation distinction. Strong conclusion. Note this PSC paper has philosophical analytic style combined with continental/postcolonial ontology — a distinctive hybrid.

title: Democratizing Civil Disobedience
author: Celikates

opening_move.description: The mainstream liberal view of civil disobedience (Rawls, Habermas) — non-violent, public, accepting of punishment, constrained by fidelity to rule of law — fails to capture civil disobedience as a genuinely political and democratic practice. It domesticates and sanitizes what is in fact an irreducibly political practice of contestation.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Civil disobedience's democratizing and politicizing potential resides precisely in the irreducible tension between its symbolic and directly confrontational dimensions — a tension that liberal theory effaces.

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper performs immanent critique of the Rawls-Habermas tradition (non-violence criterion is politically instrumentalized; acceptance of punishment is not a precondition; the appeal to majority's sense of justice is empirically questionable in Thoreau/Gandhi/King paradigm cases). It then argues for a 'radically democratic' understanding where civil disobedience sits between symbolic politics and real confrontation, and its politicizing potential is tied to this tension.

epistemic_mode.notes: Fully normative political philosophy. Historical examples (Thoreau, Gandhi, King, Wikileaks, Anonymous) are illustrative reference points used to test the adequacy of theoretical definitions, not empirical cases.

normative_stake.domain: democratic theory / political action / civil disobedience

confidence_notes: Type 2 (polemical reconstruction): immanent critique of liberal account, positive radically-democratic framework proposed, counter-objections addressed. The conclusion is open because the paper is a special section article — shorter, more programmatic, ends by flagging remaining challenges rather than fully resolving them.

title: Online Astroturfing: A Problem Beyond Disinformation
author: Chan

opening_move.description: Astroturfing is typically treated as just another form of disinformation — but this misses its distinctive mechanism: it exploits the human tendency to conform to perceived crowd behaviour. The puzzle is that the harms of astroturfing extend far beyond false information into the distortion of norm formation and crowd wisdom.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Astroturfing requires its own remedy separate from general disinformation regulation because it fundamentally undermines the epistemic infrastructure of collective decision-making through conformity manipulation.

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper accepts existing definitions of coordinated inauthentic behaviour but argues they are insufficient because they miss the conformity mechanism. It builds a framework using social psychology of conformity (pluralistic ignorance, false consensus) to show astroturfing's harms exceed disinformation — it stabilizes suboptimal norms and corrupts crowd wisdom as an epistemic tool.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Conceptual analysis drawing on social psychology of conformity; philosophical argumentation about epistemic harms.
epistemic_mode.notes: Classified as fully_normative despite references to social psychology because the psychological research is imported as conceptual resource (conformity mechanism) rather than original evidence. The argument is philosophical analysis of astroturfing's epistemic harms.

normative_stake.domain: social epistemology / digital democracy / online manipulation

confidence_notes: Type 2 (framework construction variant): the paper does not perform immanent critique of a rival theory but rather distinguishes astroturfing from adjacent phenomena and builds a novel conceptual framework for its specific harms. Closer to the TCS Type 2b (framework construction dominant) than 2a (dialectical synthesis). Analytic in style, normative in conclusion.

title: Foucault's Naturalism: The Importance of Scientific Epistemology for the Genealogical Method
author: D'Cruz

opening_move.description: Foucault's work has long been suspected of reducing knowledge to power and collapsing into relativism. These concerns presuppose a misunderstanding: Foucault does occasionally make normative epistemological judgements, and it is unclear what secures the descriptive rigour of genealogy. Two outstanding questions require resolution.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Foucault's respect for natural science and his modelling of genealogy on empirical inquiry are what secure both his normative epistemological judgements and the descriptive rigour of his method — making him a 'naturalist' in a specific sense.

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper offers a novel reconstruction of Foucault's methodology emphasising his respect for natural science (Bachelard, Canguilhem tradition). It argues Foucault's normative epistemological judgements about the human sciences presuppose a standard derived from his engagement with scientific epistemology; and genealogy's descriptive rigour is secured by modelling it on empirical inquiry.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Close reading of Foucault's methodological texts (Archaeology of Knowledge, The Order of Things, genealogical works); situating within French history and philosophy of science (Bachelard, Canguilhem, Cavaillès).
epistemic_mode.notes: This is a different paper from d-cruz-2025-what-demarcates-necropolitics which was in the TCS corpus. This PSC paper is a close internal reconstruction of Foucault's methodology. Fully normative.

normative_stake.domain: philosophy of social science / methodology / Foucault

confidence_notes: Type 2 (internal reconstruction variant): rather than critiquing rivals and proposing an alternative, the paper reconstructs what Foucault actually does to defuse the relativism objection. The normative work is methodological rather than political-normative. Strong conclusion.

title: A Marxist Reading of Ludwig Wittgenstein: Making the Case for Social and Political Change
author: Deegan

opening_move.description: Wittgenstein's famous dictum that philosophy 'leaves everything as it is' (§124) and Marx's 11th thesis on Feuerbach ('the point is to change it') appear diametrically opposed. Conventional interpretations treat §124 as a repudiation of transformative theory. The paper argues this reading is wrong.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Wittgenstein's later philosophy, properly read, encourages us to imagine alternatives and contemplate concrete possibilities for changing the world — aligning with rather than opposing Marx's transformative imperative.

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper performs an immanent reinterpretation of §124 by reading it within Wittgenstein's broader therapeutic project: philosophy leaves problematic language games as they are in order to show their contingency, which itself opens space for imagining alternatives. This aligns with existing Marxist readings (Pleasants, Kitching) and situates Wittgenstein in the philosophy of education as an advocate for critical thinking about social possibility.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Close reading of Wittgenstein's Philosophical Investigations (§§109, 124, 128); cross-reading with Marx's Theses on Feuerbach; engagement with secondary literature on Wittgenstein/Marx connections (Pleasants, Kitching, Sharrock).
epistemic_mode.notes: This paper is primarily hermeneutic: the goal is to correctly interpret §124. The normative claim (Wittgenstein advocates social change) is derived from the interpretation but the paper's argumentative centre is the interpretive reading, not an independent normative thesis. Classified as purely_interpretive_hermeneutic with a normative implication — not fully_normative because the argument cannot stand without the close reading of Wittgenstein.

normative_stake.domain: philosophy of education / critical theory / Marxism

key_concepts: philosophy leaves everything as it is
confidence_notes: Classified as purely_interpretive_hermeneutic because the argumentative core is textual interpretation of §124, not construction of an independent normative framework. The normative stake (Wittgenstein supports social change) is the hermeneutic conclusion rather than the argument's ground. This resembles featherstone-turner or loizidou from the TCS corpus but has more normative ambition — a boundary case between purely_interpretive and fully_normative.

title: Towards a Decolonial Political Theory: Thinking from the Zone of Nonbeing
author: des Portes

opening_move.description: Comparative political theory and decolonization efforts in political theory (Mills, Mignolo, Temin) remain trapped in a continental ontology and over-emphasis on methodology, reducing politics to an instrument. The puzzle: how to develop a genuinely decolonial political theory that takes politics as first philosophy?
opening_move.stakes_claimed: A decolonial existential political theory grounded in Fanon's 'zone of nonbeing' — taking the damnés (the wretched) as the political subject — provides an alternative foundation that avoids both Eurocentric universalism and purely methodological decolonization.

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper performs a partial immanent critique of existing decolonization approaches: they remain embedded in continental ontology (substance, method, epistemology) and turn decolonization into a meta-theoretical project. Against this, the paper draws on Aimé Césaire's Letter to Maurice Thorez (rejection of the French Communist Party's universalism) and Fanon's Political Theory of the Damnés to outline a decolonial existential political theory that starts from the experience of the zone of nonbeing.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Close reading of Césaire's Letter to Maurice Thorez and Fanon's Political Theory of the Damnés as philosophical texts expressing a decolonial political ontology; conceptual analysis of 'zone of nonbeing' and 'politics as first philosophy'.
epistemic_mode.notes: The cases (Césaire's letter, Fanon's political theory) are philosophical-historical texts used generatively — the decolonial political theory is derived from close reading of these texts rather than from independent conceptual construction. This qualifies as normative_with_interpretive_case in the TCS Type 3 sense: philosophical texts as generative source of the argument.

case_type.case_name: Aimé Césaire's Letter to Maurice Thorez; Fanon's Political Theory of the Damnés

normative_stake.domain: decolonial theory / political philosophy / existential politics

confidence_notes: Classified as normative_with_interpretive_case (Type 3b variant): Césaire and Fanon's texts are read as philosophical sources generating the concept of decolonial political theory, not merely as illustrations. The case_function is generative in the same way as des-portes uses these texts to build a framework that could not be constructed without them. The conclusion is research_agenda because the paper outlines a direction rather than completing the framework.

title: From Critical Theory to Critical Therapy: Towards a Permanent Psycho-Political Revolution between Subjective and Objective Disalienation
author: Dyson

opening_move.description: Critical theory and organized left politics have historically assumed an undialectical either/or between reformist therapy and revolutionary politics — anti-psychiatry dismissed therapy as domination; critical theory romanticized madness. In the context of a worsening mental health crisis under UK neoliberalism, this conceit is untenable.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Fanon's institutional psychotherapy offers a dialectical way out: recovery as disalienation requires both subjective therapeutic transformation and structural transformation of the pathogenic social world — a permanent psycho-political revolution.

rivals_engaged: Frankfurt School critical theory (Marcuse, Adorno on therapy)
care-ethical turn of the contemporary left
theoretical_positioning.description: The paper draws on a lesser-known French Freudo-Marxist psycho-political tradition (Tosquelles, Bonnafé) contemporaneous with the Frankfurt School to contest anti-psychiatric romanticism of madness. Fanon's institutional psychotherapy is the pivot: it demonstrates that therapy and structural critique are not opposed but dialectically united in the concept of disalienation.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Close reading of Fanon's institutional psychotherapy writings; genealogical reconstruction of French Freudo-Marxist tradition (Tosquelles, Bonnafé); conceptual analysis of disalienation.
epistemic_mode.notes: UK mental health statistics referenced (care crisis context) but entirely as background framing, not as evidence. The argument is fully conceptual-normative: genealogical reconstruction + immanent critique of anti-psychiatry + Fanonian synthesis.

normative_stake.domain: critical theory / psychopolitics / mental health / left politics

confidence_notes: Type 2b (genealogical deepening): the recovery of a forgotten French Freudo-Marxist tradition is the genealogical move that enables the synthesis. Fanon's institutional psychotherapy is the pivot concept. Strong conclusion specifying what 'permanent psycho-political revolution' requires.

title: The Historicisation of the Human Senses from Feuerbach to Marx
author: Engelman

opening_move.description: The German philosophical tradition (Kant, Herder, Hegel) holds that life-needs determine a life form's senses. Feuerbach and Marx retain this but add a historicist turn: human needs are historically contingent, therefore human senses are historically contingent. This challenges commonplace accounts of German philosophical anthropology (Honneth-Joas) that underestimate this historicism.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: The historicist turn in theorizing human senses — initiated by Feuerbach and developed by Marx — is philosophically significant and challenges standard accounts of Marx's relationship to Feuerbach and the philosophical-anthropological legacy of Marxian thought.

rivals_engaged: Honneth-Joas account of German philosophical anthropology
theoretical_positioning.description: The paper reconstructs the Kant-Feuerbach-Marx trajectory on sensibility/historicism to show that the historicist turn precedes Marx and that Marx's critique of Feuerbach is more nuanced than standardly presented. The immanent challenge to Honneth-Joas is that their account of German philosophical anthropology misses this historicism.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Close reading of Feuerbach's Essence of Christianity and Principles of the Philosophy of the Future; Marx's Economic and Philosophical Manuscripts of 1844, German Ideology, and Capital; Kant's anthropological writings; Herder.
epistemic_mode.notes: Philosophical scholarship/history of ideas. The paper traces a conceptual development rather than advancing a normative thesis. The normative implication (this historicism matters for Marxian theory) is secondary to the interpretive reconstruction. Classified as purely_interpretive_hermeneutic.

normative_stake.domain: history of philosophy / Marxian theory / German philosophical anthropology

confidence_notes: Classified as purely_interpretive_hermeneutic: the paper is philosophical scholarship/history of ideas. Its primary mode is genealogical reconstruction of a philosophical concept (historicization of the senses) rather than normative argument. The challenge to Honneth-Joas is scholarly correction, not political-normative prescription.

title: Marcuse's Critique of Technology Today
author: Feenberg

opening_move.description: Marcuse's critique of science and technology was eclipsed with the collapse of the New Left whose theoretician he was. Yet it is newly relevant in the context of impending environmental catastrophe due to climate change. The puzzle: why has Marcuse been neglected, and what does his work actually offer for today's ecological crisis?
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Marcuse's experiential ontology — derived from his phenomenological background and Marxist critique of instrumental reason — contains valuable resources for contemporary environmental struggles unavailable in other Frankfurt School thinkers.

rivals_engaged: Horkheimer and Adorno's early critique of instrumental reason (Dialectic of Enlightenment)
theoretical_positioning.description: The paper reconstructs Marcuse's development from early phenomenological reading of Marx's 1844 Manuscripts through Eros and Civilization to One-Dimensional Man, arguing his unique contribution was a phenomenological critique of technology as reification of nature. It argues Marcuse extended Horkheimer-Adorno's critique most fully and that this extension is newly politically relevant.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Reconstruction of Marcuse's intellectual development; close reading of One-Dimensional Man and early Marxist writings; comparison with Horkheimer-Adorno.
epistemic_mode.notes: Reconstruction of Marcuse's thought as resource for contemporary ecological politics. Fully normative — the climate change references are background motivation, not evidential material.

normative_stake.domain: critical theory / technology critique / environmentalism

confidence_notes: Type 2b (genealogical deepening): the paper's primary function is recovery/reconstruction of Marcuse's neglected work rather than critique of rivals or proposal of a new framework. The normative derivation (Marcuse is valuable for environmental politics today) is the conclusion of the reconstruction rather than an independently argued claim. Open conclusion.

title: Jean Améry and the Time of Resentment
author: Ferber

opening_move.description: Jean Améry's essay 'Resentments' (1966) is firmly grounded in his Holocaust survival experience, but it also articulates a philosophy of temporality — specifically, a diagnosis of how natural time's forward movement (forgetting, forgiving, moving on) enacts a moral injustice toward victims.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Resentment is not a psychological defect but a moral-temporal claim: the refusal to let natural time undo the irreversibility of injury, and an insistence that the past must retain its claim on the present and future.

rivals_engaged: Nietzsche's account of eternal recurrence and resentment
theoretical_positioning.description: The paper is a close reading of Améry's 'Resentments' structured around five temporal dimensions: delay, eternal recurrence (critique of Nietzsche), natural time and forgiveness, moral time and irreversibility, and the future. It reconstructs Améry's philosophy of temporality as a counter to the Nietzschean account of resentment as weakness.

data_sources: Améry's 'Resentments' essay from At the Mind's Limits (1966)
epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Close reading of Améry's essay structured around temporal dimensions; comparative analysis with Nietzsche on resentment and eternal recurrence.
epistemic_mode.notes: The essay text is treated as a philosophical-literary text constitutive of the argument. The paper's normative claim (resentment as legitimate moral-temporal claim) cannot be made without the close reading of Améry — the text is generative. This is Type 3b (philosophical-historical corpus as parable): Améry's autobiographical-philosophical essay generates a philosophy of temporality and justice.

case_type.case_name: Jean Améry, 'Resentments' (essay in At the Mind's Limits, 1966)

normative_stake.domain: philosophy of memory / Holocaust / justice / temporality

confidence_notes: Type 3b (philosophical text as parable): close reading of a single philosophical-literary essay generates a philosophy of temporality with normative implications for justice and responsibility. The Nietzsche engagement is an immanent critique embedded in the case development: Améry's critique of eternal recurrence as applied to historical injustice. Classified as normative_with_interpretive_case because the case (Améry's essay) is constitutive of the framework.

title: Rethinking Utopias through Hannah Arendt: Youth Climate Activism and the Politics of Possibility
author: Firinci Orman

opening_move.description: Youth climate activism (Fridays for Future, Greta Thunberg) exhibits an open-ended, relational quality that static utopian frameworks cannot capture. The paper proposes rethinking utopias as spaces of ongoing political engagement rather than fixed visions of a perfect society, using Arendt's political theory.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Viewing youth climate activism through Arendt's concepts (natality, freedom, plurality, action) reveals it as a form of politics of possibility — cultivating conditions for freedom in the present rather than pursuing a distant utopia.

It extends Arendt by bringing her into dialogue with climate activism scholarship and the literature on prefigurative politics.

title: The Platformization of the Public Sphere and its Challenge to Democracy
author: Fischer and Jarren

title: The Neoliberal Influence on South Africa's Early Democracy and its Shortfalls in Addressing Economic Inequality
author: Fourie

opening_move.description: Post-Apartheid South Africa has become one of the world's most unequal countries despite political liberation. The paper investigates how neoliberal economic policies (RDP, then GEAR) adopted by the ANC government contributed to deepening rather than reducing economic inequality.

The paper reads the RDP-to-GEAR transition as adoption of neoliberal political rationality, not merely economic pragmatism, and diagnoses its failure to address structural inequality.

title: Antonio Negri, Spinoza, Marx, and Digital Capitalism
author: Fuchs

opening_move.description: Spinoza's philosophy has been used by Negri to analyse capitalism and the working class, but it has not been adequately applied to digital capitalism specifically. The paper asks: how can Spinoza help us better understand digital capitalism, particularly the antagonism between digital labour and digital capital?

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper critically and constructively engages Negri's Spinoza interpretation, combining it with Marx's class analysis to produce a framework for digital capitalism. It maps Spinoza's potentia/potestas dialectic onto digital labour/capital antagonism, digital commons/commodities, and productive forces/relations of production.

title: For a Negative Hermeneutics: Adorno, Gadamer and Critical Consciousness
author: Giannakakis

opening_move.description: The present social-historical moment is marked by a communication breakdown between subject and object, humanity and nature. A 'historically adequate consciousness' is needed. The paper investigates whether the intersection of Adorno's philosophical interpretation and Gadamer's philosophical hermeneutics can generate such a consciousness.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: A 'negative hermeneutics' — combining Adorno's immanent critique and Gadamer's dialogical enculturation, mediated by the concept of mimesis — may be what is needed to foster alternative ways of thinking and transform (or spare) the world.

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper reads Adorno and Gadamer as two forms of protest against instrumental rationality: Adorno through radical historicism and immanent critique; Gadamer through dialogical tradition. It maps convergences (immanent vs transcended critique, tradition vs experience) and divergences, proposing 'negative hermeneutics' as a synthesis oriented to critical consciousness and social transformation.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Comparative philosophical reading of Adorno (Negative Dialectics, Minima Moralia) and Gadamer (Truth and Method); conceptual analysis of mimesis, experience, tradition.
epistemic_mode.notes: Fully normative: philosophical synthesis of two traditions. The 'negative hermeneutics' proposal is programmatic (conclusion is open/inconclusive). The paper acknowledges at the end that reflections are 'suggestive yet inconclusive'.

normative_stake.domain: critical theory / hermeneutics / critical consciousness / culture

confidence_notes: Type 2 (synthesis variant): two positions mapped and synthesized into 'negative hermeneutics'. The reflexive_move is true because the paper explicitly acknowledges the inconclusiveness of its synthesis in the conclusion. Open conclusion.

title: Patterns of Withdrawal: Adorno and the Beats

opening_move.description: Both Adorno's negative dialectics and the Beat Generation emerged as radical responses to post-WWII social and ideological landscapes, united by scepticism toward instrumental rationality and shared dedication to non-identity, dissonance, and withdrawal. The puzzle: do these apparently disparate traditions share a structural logic of critical disaffiliation?
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Adorno's theoretical critique of late-capitalist reality and the Beats' literary/performative rejection of bourgeois norms converge in a shared endeavour of critical disaffiliation — negation and withdrawal as active forms of critique, not passive retreat.

epistemic_mode.qualitative_methods: Close reading of Adorno's Negative Dialectics and Minima Moralia alongside Beat literary texts (Howl, On the Road, Doctor Sax); comparative cultural-philosophical analysis.

The case is generative: the parallel reading generates the concept of 'critical disaffiliation' as a shared pattern. title: Self-Esteem and Competition
author: Gilabert

opening_move.description: Self-esteem is a very important good; competition is widespread and commonly linked to self-esteem (people seek it through competitive success). But empirical research suggests competition has a strong tendency to undermine self-esteem. How might we foster a desirable distribution of self-esteem in the face of difficulties created by competition?
anchored_in: empirical psychology of self-esteem and competition
opening_move.stakes_claimed: A solidaristic path — identifying mechanisms that support individuals' self-esteem while imposing limits on competition — is preferable to either self-denial or uncritical celebration of the rat race.

Empirical studies on competition and self-esteem

title: Contra Carl Schmitt: A Play-Centered Alternative to his Existentially Distorted Concept of Politics
author: Gómez Albarello

opening_move.description: Schmitt's conceptualization of the political as the friend-enemy distinction has attained notorious influence, but this analysis rests on biased and skewed phenomenological analysis. The paper diagnoses Schmitt's framework as an existentialist distortion of modern political experience through the metaphors of enmity and war.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: A play-centered alternative concept of politics — derived from Huizinga's homo ludens framework — provides a more adequate and less distorted account of the political, where politics is norm-bounded competition rather than existential enmity.

Drawing on Huizinga's play theory, the paper proposes a norm-bounded concept of politics as an alternative.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Close reading of Schmitt's Concept of the Political; engagement with Huizinga's Homo Ludens; conceptual analysis of political metaphors.
epistemic_mode.notes: Fully normative: immanent critique of Schmitt followed by Huizinga-based alternative. The phenomenological reading of political experience is conceptual analysis, not empirical.

normative_stake.domain: political philosophy / theory of politics / Schmitt / play

title: The Violence Inherent in the System
author: Heath

opening_move.description: Monty Python's satire of 'the violence inherent in the system' has led left-wing theorists to abandon the underlying social critique. But the theory being satirized — that all social practices necessarily rely on symbolic violence — has continued to exercise influence on protest culture despite never receiving explicit philosophical articulation.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Explicitly articulating the theory shows that it falls short of providing a meaningful critique of any aspect of social practices — it proves too much and explains too little.

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper reconstructs the implicit theory of systemic violence in left protest culture, shows it derives from a confused generalization of Weber's monopoly on violence, and argues the theory fails because (a) it proves too much (all social norms involve coercion), (b) it has implausible implications for protest (all deviation is justified), and (c) it generates a performative contradiction.

epistemic_mode.notes: Fully normative analytical critique. The Monty Python reference and protest examples are rhetorical/illustrative, not empirical.

normative_stake.domain: political philosophy / protest / violence / collective action

confidence_notes: Type 2 (polemical reconstruction): explicit articulation of an implicit theory followed by thorough immanent critique showing it collapses. Strong conclusion. Unusual opening (Monty Python) but standard Type 2 argument structure. The paper has a contrarian tone aimed at left intellectual culture — consistent with analytic political philosophy style within PSC.

title: Understanding and Evaluating Populist Strategy
author: Jenkins

opening_move.description: Existing accounts define populism by a specific set of tactics (hijacking institutions, clientelism, friend-enemy rhetoric) supposedly flowing directly from populist ideas. But this conflates the ideational and strategic levels — the same orientation can justify very different tactics. The puzzle: how do we identify populist strategy without falling into a fixed tactics-based definition?
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Populist strategy is best understood as whatever range of tactics political actors use who accept a specific orientation: that elite-people enmity defines political life, creates expanded moral permissions, and requires resisting elite revanchism. This orientation, not any specific tactic, is what makes strategy populist.

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper critiques tactical definitions of populism (immanent critique: they mistake contingent expressions for necessary features) and constructs an orientation-based account: populist strategy flows from three components of the populist orientation (people-elite enmity, institutional ambivalence, elite revanchism), which justify a flexible range of strategies.

epistemic_mode.notes: Fully normative analytical political philosophy. References to populist movements (Chávez, Trump, Orbán) are illustrative examples, not empirical cases.

normative_stake.domain: political theory / populism / democratic theory

confidence_notes: Type 2a (dialectical synthesis): critique of tactical-definition approaches, positive orientation-based account proposed. Strong conclusion. Analytic political philosophy style.

title: Work of Art in the Age of Its AI Reproduction
author: Kalpokas

opening_move.description: Benjamin's analysis of mechanical reproduction is inadequate for AI-generated art: AI generation involves identification and inventive representation of data patterns, not mere mechanical surface mimicry. How should we theorize AI art in Benjaminian terms?
opening_move.stakes_claimed: AI-generated art occupies a unique position: it exceeds mere mechanical reproduction (accessing the 'collective unconscious' of society through data patterns) while simultaneously maximizing exhibition value and distraction — making it 'perfect for the age of increasing distraction'.

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper extends Benjamin's framework by distinguishing AI generation from mechanical reproduction: whereas photography and film mechanically represent the world as presented to a device, AI involves pattern identification from data. This gives AI art a paradoxical status: partial recovery of auratic capacity (through collective unconscious access) combined with maximum exhibition value and distraction.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Close reading of Benjamin's 'Work of Art in the Age of Mechanical Reproducibility'; conceptual application to AI-generated art; philosophical analysis of data pattern recognition as distinct from mechanical reproduction.
epistemic_mode.notes: Benjamin's essay functions as the interpretive case from which the argument is derived. The paper's position is generated by close reading of Benjamin and extending his framework — it cannot stand without the Benjaminian close reading. case_type.case_name: Walter Benjamin, 'Work of Art in the Age of Its Mechanical Reproducibility'

normative_stake.domain: aesthetics / AI / critical theory / culture

confidence_notes: Type 3b (philosophical text as generative source): Benjamin's essay is the constitutive case from which the distinction between AI art and mechanical reproduction is derived. The paper's argument cannot be reconstructed without the close Benjamin reading.

title: Adorno's Negative Dialectics and Its Debt to Nietzsche: Could Nietzsche Be the Originator of Adorno's Negative Dialectics?
author: Kastrinakis

opening_move.description: The dominant view holds that Nietzsche's influence on Adorno is primarily visible in Minima Moralia (style, aphorism). But Adorno himself declared he owes Nietzsche 'by far the greatest debt — more even than Hegel'. This article investigates whether Nietzsche could be the originator of Adorno's Negative Dialectics.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Nietzsche's most significant contribution to Adorno's thought is to be found in Negative Dialectics (non-identity thinking, critique of identity thinking) rather than in Minima Moralia — a claim that would overturn the dominant Rose-influenced view.

rivals_engaged: Gillian Rose's account of Adorno-Nietzsche (Minima Moralia focus)
theoretical_positioning.description: The paper systematically traces Nietzschean themes in Negative Dialectics: critique of identity thinking, non-identity as philosophical imperative, style as critique of systematic philosophy, suffering as philosophical catalyst. It argues these are structural debts to Nietzsche, not stylistic borrowings confined to Minima Moralia.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Close reading of Adorno's Negative Dialectics and Minima Moralia alongside Nietzsche's Beyond Good and Evil, Genealogy of Morals, and Untimely Meditations; comparison with Rose's account.
epistemic_mode.notes: Primarily philosophical scholarship/history of ideas — traces textual influences and structural parallels between Nietzsche and Adorno. The normative implication (this changes our understanding of Adorno's method) is secondary to the interpretive claim. Classified as purely_interpretive_hermeneutic.

normative_stake.domain: history of philosophy / critical theory / Adorno / Nietzsche

confidence_notes: Purely_interpretive_hermeneutic: philosophical scholarship tracing textual and structural influences. Strong conclusion (Nietzsche is the originator) but the 'normative' content is a scholarly interpretive claim, not a political-normative prescription.

title: Habermas and the Mutations of the Public Sphere
author: Kellner

opening_move.description: Habermas's public sphere problematic deserves critical scrutiny in the contemporary moment of global democratic crisis. But existing engagements have not adequately updated his framework for the digital age and contemporary social movements (BLM, MeToo, Ukraine solidarity).
anchored_in: Habermas (Structural Transformation of the Public Sphere)
opening_move.stakes_claimed: A concept of the public sphere that facilitates maximum public participation and debate — encompassing digital and social movement dimensions — promotes the cause of radical democracy and social transformation.

rivals_engaged: purely normative readings ignoring empirical mutations
theoretical_positioning.description: The paper reconstructs Habermas's public sphere theory historically (STPS 1962 through later Habermas) and extends it to encompass digital media transformations and contemporary social movements. It argues Habermas's problematic remains relevant but requires updating for the digital era.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Reconstruction of Habermas's intellectual development; engagement with critical responses and updating.

normative_stake.domain: public sphere / democratic theory / Habermas

title: The Public Sphere in the Mode of Systematically Distorted Communication
author: Kempf

opening_move.description: Contemporary proliferation of filter bubbles and echo chambers seems to render obsolete the concept of a public sphere in the singular. Habermas himself interprets fragmentation as colonization of the lifeworld. But this colonization reading misses an internal dialectic of communication refusal/overcoming already present in Habermas's earlier concept of 'systematically distorted communication'.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: The fragmentation of the public sphere can be theorized and politically addressed as a precarious standstill in a dialectic between communication refusal and its overcoming — a more internally coherent Habermasian position than the colonization model.

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper performs a partial immanent critique of later Habermas (colonization model) by recovering his earlier concept of systematically distorted communication. It argues the public sphere emerges from the lifeworld as the concomitant horizon of communicative action — and fragmentation is an internal dialectical moment (refusal of discourse) rather than external colonization.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Internal reconstruction of Habermas's different theoretical phases; conceptual analysis of systematically distorted communication vs. colonization.
epistemic_mode.notes: Fully normative: internal Habermas reconstruction. Part of the public sphere special issue.

normative_stake.domain: public sphere / democratic theory / communicative action

confidence_notes: Type 2 (internal reconstruction via immanent critique of later Habermas): the paper diagnoses an inconsistency between early and later Habermas and recovers the earlier concept. Open conclusion.

title: Capitalism and Contested Publicity: A Conversation with Nancy Fraser
author: Kempf and Sevignani

The interview is positioned as continuing a workshop discussion with Fraser in June 2022.

Kempf and Sevignani provide framing and questions.

Empirical references to platforms and political movements are illustrative. The framing essay is normative argument by Kempf and Sevignani; Fraser's answers constitute the theoretical content.
