title: A Semblance of Freedom: Horkheimer and Adorno's Conception of Myth
author: Kent

opening_move.description: The standard pessimistic reading of Dialectic of Enlightenment holds that reason is fully ensnared within the domination of myth, with no exit. Kent opens by diagnosing this as a misreading that misses a key dimension of Horkheimer and Adorno's argument: the possibility of liberation from myth's heteronomy via critique of myth itself. Hulatt's reinterpretation of mimesis is introduced as the key to unlocking this dimension.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Myth, understood through its linguistic mediation of human experience, is not only a form of heteronomy but also an 'incomplete but largely untapped mediator of the history of human experience' — a resource for rational self-knowledge and thus for renewed enlightenment.

rivals_engaged: pessimistic readers of Dialectic of Enlightenment (totalistic critique of reason)
Freudian reading (magic and myth as primitive irrationality)
theoretical_positioning.description: The paper performs immanent critique of the standard pessimistic reading of Horkheimer and Adorno by showing that the very text that grounds this reading contains a counter-dimension. Using Hulatt's reinterpretation of Adorno's mimesis theory (tri-partite conflict between reason, self-preservation, and mimesis), Kent argues that myth's linguistic deposit of human experience enables a critique of domination from within. The paper thus reconstructs an immanent emancipatory potential in Dialectic of Enlightenment.

epistemic_mode.qualitative_methods: Close reading of Dialectic of Enlightenment (Horkheimer and Adorno) through the lens of Hulatt's reconstruction of Adorno's mimesis theory. Philological comparison of key passages. Secondary engagement with Caillois, Mauss, Freud on myth and mimesis.
data_sources: Horkheimer and Adorno, Dialectic of Enlightenment
Hulatt, 'Reason, Mimesis, and Self-Preservation in Adorno'
epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Genealogical reconstruction: traces the argument of Dialectic of Enlightenment regarding myth-mimesis-language nexus; uses Hulatt as corrective lens for standard reading; builds from this to a positive account of myth's cognitive-critical potential.
epistemic_mode.notes: This paper has no explicit normative conclusion about what ought to be done in the present — it reconstructs a theoretical position within Horkheimer and Adorno's text. The normative implication (enlightenment remains possible via myth critique) is drawn out but not programmatically stated. This makes it purely_interpretive_hermeneutic rather than normative_with_interpretive_case. Unlike kastrinakis_2024 (which asks whether Nietzsche is an 'originator' of Adorno), this paper uses interpretation to rescue an emancipatory potential — but stops short of deriving a normative claim for the present. Closest to the Dialectic of Enlightenment exegesis tradition.

normative_stake.domain: Frankfurt School / critical theory / enlightenment / myth

confidence_notes: Kent's paper is classified purely_interpretive_hermeneutic because the entire argument concerns the correct reading of Horkheimer and Adorno's Dialectic of Enlightenment. There is no contemporary normative application or programmatic conclusion. The stakes remain within textual-philosophical interpretation. The move_sequence includes normative_derivation because Kent does derive a positive thesis about myth's emancipatory potential — but this derives from text-internal analysis, not from a normative argument about the present. This distinguishes it from giannakakis_2026 (which uses Adorno interpretively to ground a normative claim about withdrawal and critical culture).

title: Fugitive Freedom and Radical Care: Towards a Standpoint Theory of Normativity
author: Loick

opening_move.description: Opening with Césaire's 'boomerang' metaphor for the moral corruption that domination inflicts on the dominator, the paper diagnoses a gap in standpoint theory: while epistemic standpoint theories are well-developed (Mills, Fricker), there is no parallel standpoint theory of normativity. The puzzle: if domination distorts not only epistemic but also normative capacities, oppressed groups may have normative advantages — but this has not been theorized.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: A normative standpoint theory grounded in two heterogeneous traditions — the Black Radical Tradition (fugitive freedom) and feminist care ethics — can provide a theory of how political struggle enables oppressed groups to develop superior normative orientations.

rivals_engaged: universalist ethics that ignore social situatedness
theoretical_positioning.description: The paper extends and synthesizes. It extends epistemic standpoint theory (Mills' 'moral ignorance' thesis) into a normative domain. It synthesizes two traditions — the Black Radical Tradition on fugitive freedom (Harriet Tubman, Frederick Douglass, Angela Davis) and feminist care ethics (Gilligan, Noddings, Held) — arguing both are grounded in similar presuppositions (political struggle provoked by social contradictions). The paper builds a framework by identifying three aspects: passive conditions enabling normative advantage; active development through struggle; translation into emancipatory practice.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Conceptual-genealogical: reads key texts of the Black Radical Tradition (Tubman, Douglass, Davis) and feminist care ethics as philosophical sources for theorizing normative standpoints. Uses Hegel's master-servant dialectic as structural resource.
epistemic_mode.notes: The paper engages historical figures and practices (Harriet Tubman's Underground Railroad, feminist care theory) not as empirical cases but as philosophical resources for conceptual construction. This is fully_normative because the argument moves entirely within conceptual and normative space — the examples are philosophical touchstones, not empirical evidence.

normative_stake.domain: normative theory / standpoint theory / critical theory / abolition / care ethics

confidence_notes: Classified fully_normative because the references to Tubman, Douglass, and care practice are conceptual resources for framework construction, not empirical cases. The move_sequence includes case_introduction/case_development because these traditions are introduced and developed as philosophical materials — analogous to how other fully_normative papers use thinkers as conceptual resources. The paper is a Type 2b variant (reconstruction of two traditions into a new synthesis) but with a novel architecture: synthesizing Black Radical Tradition + feminist care ethics into a standpoint theory of normativity. This makes it distinctive within the PSC corpus.

title: Against Institutional Racism
author: Matthew

opening_move.description: Opening by diagnosing that contemporary discussions of racism have shifted to 'institutional' and 'structural' racism, Matthew immediately states his counter-thesis: much so-called institutional racism is not racism at all, and this extra-individual focus is a mistake that obscures the continuing importance of individual racism. The paper signals a polemical, revisionary stance from the first paragraph.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Contemporary discussions of race should return to a significant focus on individuals; institutional racism as typically theorized (especially extrinsic/disparate impact versions) lacks a plausible account of what makes institutions racist; Sally Haslanger's structural approach faces serious objections.

rivals_engaged: extrinsic institutional racism / disparate impact conception
theoretical_positioning.description: The paper distinguishes intrinsic institutional racism (endorsed) from extrinsic/disparate impact institutional racism (rejected). It accepts that García's affective individual model can explain intrinsic institutional racism. It then systematically critiques extrinsic institutional racism: (1) no plausible account of what makes such institutions racist; (2) overstated explanatory indispensability — individual racism is at least as important; (3) Haslanger's structural account fails to ground the relevant normative claims; (4) institutional reform may be insufficient even if accepted. The paper defends a return to individual-level analysis.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Analytical philosophy of race: precise conceptual distinctions (intrinsic vs extrinsic institutional racism), scenario analysis (S1–S4 cases), immanent critique of rival accounts through internal objections.
epistemic_mode.notes: Classic analytic political philosophy mode: argument proceeds through conceptual distinctions, hypothetical scenarios, and internal critique. No empirical evidence; occasional reference to real-world cases (police killings of Black people) as motivating context, not evidential basis. This is a polemical reconstruction arguing for a minority position against the dominant framing of racism discourse.

normative_stake.domain: philosophy of race / racism / political philosophy

confidence_notes: A distinctive paper in the PSC corpus: it argues against the dominant critical race theory framing, defending an individualist and analytic account. This is a Type 2a variant (polemical reconstruction with strong thesis) but unusual in its direction — most PSC papers argue for more structural, critical, or decolonial positions. Matthew argues for less structural analysis. The move_sequence follows a tight analytic pattern: conceptual_distinction → rival_mapping → immanent_critique → counter_objection → conclusion. No case; no empirical evidence. Classified fully_normative. High confidence in classification.

title: Cin ciun cian (ching chong): Yellowness and Neo-Orientalism in Italy at the Time of COVID-19
author: Miyake

opening_move.description: Opens with the UN Secretary General's description of COVID-19 as an 'x-ray' revealing social fractures, then presents empirical data on the rise of anti-Asian racism in Italy in early 2020: police reports, Pew Research Center data, Stop AAPI Hate statistics, media documentation of 'cin ciun cian' mockery and slit-eye poses. The paper positions the Italian case as a paradigm for theorizing 'yellowness' as a form of bio-cultural racialization.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: The Italian case reveals how global and local trajectories overlap to reproduce neo-orientalism via 'yellowness' — a racialized category intersecting somatic, cultural, and civilizational markers — and how this process has been both enacted and contested in a contemporary national context.

rivals_engaged: culturalist rather than structural approaches to racism
theoretical_positioning.description: The paper introduces 'yellowness' as an original analytical concept: a bio-cultural racialization process constructing 'yellow/Oriental/Chinese' otherness, intersecting with whiteness and blackness in a hierarchical racialization order. Drawing on critical race theory (Bonilla-Silva), postcolonial studies (Said, Hall), and neo-orientalism literature, the paper constructs a conceptual framework for understanding how contemporary Italian anti-Asian racism reproduces orientalist tropes under pandemic conditions.

epistemic_mode.qualitative_methods: Media discourse analysis: examination of Italian newspaper coverage, social media posts, political statements, the 'cin ciun cian' mock-language and slit-eye gesture as discourse units. Historical contextualization of Italian colonial relations with East Asia and legacy of Orientalism. Reference to interview/testimony evidence from anti-racist organizations.
data_sources: social media documentation of racist incidents
historical colonial archives on Italian Orientalism
epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Critical discourse analysis: reading media texts as ideological sites for reproducing neo-orientalist constructions of 'yellowness'. Intersectional analysis: how yellowness articulates with whiteness, class, and gender in Italian context. Historical genealogy of Yellow Peril trope.
epistemic_mode.notes: The empirical evidence is constitutive: the paper cannot make its argument about 'yellowness' in contemporary Italy without the media discourse analysis of Italian COVID-era racism. This is normative_with_qualitative_empirical (constitutive role) rather than purely empirical because the paper draws normative conclusions about what the case reveals about ongoing colonialist assumptions and the need for decolonial anti-racist politics.

case_type.case_name: Italian COVID-19 anti-Asian racism (early 2020) — media discourse and racist incidents

normative_stake.domain: race / Orientalism / colonialism / contemporary Italy / pandemic politics

confidence_notes: This paper is normative_with_qualitative_empirical with constitutive evidence role. The Italian case is central and cannot be removed without destroying the argument. The concept of 'yellowness' is developed through and from the case analysis. The move_sequence begins empirically (evidence presentation) before moving to normative framework construction — similar to fourie_2022's pattern. Closure is open: the paper ends with analysis of contestation and solidarity practices, without a programmatic prescription. The conceptual contribution ('yellowness' as a distinct racialization form) distinguishes it from purely descriptive case studies.

title: Socialist Democracy: Rosa Luxemburg's Challenge to Democratic Theory
author: Muldoon and Booth

opening_move.description: Opening from a diagnosis of mainstream democratic theory's blind spot: since the end of the Cold War, democratic theory has presupposed the compatibility of liberal democracy with capitalism, neglecting economic considerations. Luxemburg is introduced as an under-utilized resource who contests this consensus — arguing that liberal capitalist democracy is structurally unable to realize its own ideals of freedom and equality.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Luxemburg offers a distinctive model of socialist democracy that extends democratic principles to economic institutions, responding to both liberal objections (property rights) and Marxist objections (the state). This model is relevant today despite changed conditions.

rivals_engaged: liberal democratic theory (Dahl, Habermas, deliberative democracy)
theoretical_positioning.description: The paper recovers Luxemburg's institutionalist socialist democracy through interpretive re-reading against standard Luxemburg reception (revolutionary socialist, anti-institutionalist). It argues that Luxemburg's pluralist, multi-party, civil-libertarian socialism offers a model superior to both liberal capitalism (which abandons economic democracy) and Marxist vanguardism (which abandons democratic institutions). The reconstruction is explicitly noted as departing from traditional interpretations by emphasizing the institutional dimension of her thought.

data_sources: Luxemburg's writings (Reform or Revolution, The Russian Revolution, Mass Strike, Letters from Prison)
contextual historical material on early 20th century socialist movements
epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Intellectual history and political theory: close reading of Luxemburg's texts, reconstruction of her institutional vision, updating for contemporary conditions. Engagement with secondary Luxemburg scholarship (Schulman, O'Kane, Bonnell, Bevir).
epistemic_mode.notes: The paper uses Luxemburg's writings as philosophical resources for normative theory construction, not as empirical case studies. The 'case' of Luxemburg's thought is a philosophical text case — but unlike des_portes or ferber, the reading is not interpretive-case driving a normative argument: it is reconstruction of a thinker's normative position to deploy in contemporary democratic theory. Classified fully_normative.

normative_stake.domain: democratic theory / socialism / political economy

confidence_notes: The reflexive_move is true: the paper explicitly notes that its institutionalist reading of Luxemburg 'may strike some as odd given the institutional dimensions of her writings are consciously underdeveloped' — acknowledging the interpretive departure and its limits. The counter_objection_response addresses both liberal objections (property rights violation) and Marxist objections (state retention). Classified fully_normative because Luxemburg's writings serve as philosophical argument material, not empirical case. This is a Type 2b (reconstruction of a canonical thinker's thought for contemporary normative application) — parallel to feenberg_2023 (Marcuse), dyson_2024 (Fanon), kellner_2024 (Habermas).

title: The Platform Economy's Infrastructural Transformation of the Public Sphere: Facebook and Cambridge Analytica Revisited
author: Nosthoff and Maschewski

opening_move.description: Opens with Andrew Bosworth's (Facebook VP) 2019 internal memo on Cambridge Analytica and Trump's 2016 campaign, which simultaneously dismisses CA as 'snake oil salespeople' and admits Facebook itself was 'responsible for Donald Trump getting elected.' This empirical entry point frames the paper's central diagnosis: the scandal was not CA's targeted advertising but Facebook's platform functioning as an 'affective infrastructure' for opinion formation.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: The CA scandal reveals the continuation and intensification of the structural transformation of the public sphere: Habermas's 1960s diagnosis of economic fabrication of the public sphere is now advanced in a more radical form through the algorithmic architecture of social media, leading to a new form of 'infrastructural power'.

rivals_engaged: binary CA-focused debates (scandal as exceptional vs normal)
theoretical_positioning.description: The paper argues that CA's practices were 'standard campaign practices' — their scandal value obscures the more significant issue of Facebook's platform architecture. Drawing on Habermas's structural transformation, Zuboff's surveillance capitalism, and cybernetics theory (Wiener, Parisi), the paper constructs a theoretical account of Facebook as 'affective infrastructure' that combines surveillance-capitalist commercialization with a cybernetic logic of control — constituting 'feedback propaganda' and algorithmic user engagement.

epistemic_mode.qualitative_methods: Media-theoretical case reconstruction: analysis of Cambridge Analytica's operational procedures from leaked documents, whistleblower accounts, investigative journalism, and SCL/CA materials. Analysis of Facebook's platform architecture, ad targeting system, algorithmic feedback loops, and political campaigning practices from public documentation.
data_sources: Cambridge Analytica / SCL internal materials (via Christopher Wylie, Guardian/NYT)
Zuboff's surveillance capitalism framework applied to case
epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Media-theoretical analysis: reading Cambridge Analytica and Facebook as media-technical phenomena within a socio-theoretical framework. Extends Habermas's structural transformation thesis to digital platform context.
epistemic_mode.notes: The paper uses Cambridge Analytica and Facebook as a contemporary case to ground a normative diagnostic argument about the transformation of the public sphere. The empirical evidence is constitutive — the theoretical claims about 'affective infrastructure' and 'feedback propaganda' are derived from the case analysis, not merely illustrated by it. This is a Type 5 variant: empirically anchored critical diagnosis, similar to fischer_jarren_2023 and kempf_2023.

case_type.case_name: Cambridge Analytica / Facebook political advertising (2016 Trump campaign; 2018 scandal)

normative_stake.domain: public sphere / digital capitalism / political communication / democracy

key_concepts: structural transformation of the public sphere
confidence_notes: This paper belongs to the PSC public sphere special issue cluster (same issue as brunkhorst et al., fischer_jarren, kempf_sevignani, kempf_2023, trenz_2023). The case is constitutive: the CA/Facebook analysis drives the theoretical argument. Epistemic mode is normative_with_qualitative_empirical (constitutive) — closest to fischer_jarren in the corpus. The paper's move_sequence follows the empirical-first pattern: opens with case presentation, derives theoretical framework from it, then draws normative conclusions about public sphere transformation. High confidence in classification.

title: Arendt and the Social: A Modern Flaw?
author: Piscitello

opening_move.description: Opens by presenting Habermas's charge that Arendt's political/social distinction 'leads to absurdities' and condemns her to anachronism. The puzzle: Arendt insists socioeconomic issues should be divorced from politics, but this seems nonsensical today when socioeconomics are the core of politics. Piscitello's counter: Habermas misreads Arendt, and her distinction is not anachronistic but a diagnosis of modernity's 'sick heart.'
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Arendt's political/social distinction, properly understood through 'respect' and 'self-containedness' of action (derived from Aristotle's political friendship and self-contained praxis), reveals the modern erosion of public space and points toward recovery of genuine political participation free from economic domination — exemplified by Musk and Trump as contemporary anti-political forces.

rivals_engaged: standard criticisms of Arendt's political/social distinction (Benhabib, Bernstein, Pitkin)
theoretical_positioning.description: The paper performs immanent critique of Habermas's reading of Arendt, arguing he misreads her by ignoring two key features: 'respect' (derived from Aristotelian political friendship) and the 'self-containedness' of action (derived from Aristotle's praxis as end-in-itself). These features show that Arendt's goal is not to replicate the ancient polis but to diagnose modernity's absorption of the public by economic necessity. Contemporary figures (Trump, Musk) serve as illustrative examples of Arendt's diagnosis.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Philosophical reconstruction: close reading of Arendt's The Human Condition and On Revolution; engagement with Habermas's 1977 critique; Aristotelian analysis of philia politike and self-contained praxis. Contemporary examples (Trump's market manipulation, Musk's public sphere colonization) used as illustrations.
epistemic_mode.notes: Trump and Musk appear as contemporary illustrations of Arendt's diagnosis, not as empirical cases. They are invoked to show the relevance of her distinction in about 2-3 paragraphs; the bulk of the argument is philosophical reconstruction and immanent critique of Habermas. Classified fully_normative. The paper is an analytic-continental hybrid: treats Arendt and Habermas via close philosophical reading with some analytic precision in conceptual distinctions.

normative_stake.domain: political theory / Arendt / public sphere / democracy

confidence_notes: Straightforward fully_normative Type 2a paper: puzzle_diagnosis + immanent critique of Habermas + reconstruction of Arendt + normative conclusion. The brief references to Trump and Musk are illustrative asides (3 paragraphs) in a paper that is otherwise entirely philosophical. The paper's brevity (16 pages) and tight argumentative structure are characteristic of the analytic-political philosophy mode in PSC. Compare piscitello to celikates_2016 for structural similarity: both use immanent critique to defend a revised normative concept (civil disobedience / political action) against its critics.

title: Black Male Disposability and the Dark Side of Canadian Multiculturalism
author: Ruwe

opening_move.description: Opens with the 2016 UN Working Group of Experts on People of African Descent report on Canada, documenting stark racial disparities in education, poverty, and criminal justice outcomes for African Canadians. The puzzle: given Canada's reputation for multiculturalism and diversity, how do Black males experience such systematic disadvantage? The paper introduces the Racial Subjugated Male Thesis (RSMT) as the analytical framework.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Black male disposability is not a policy failure of Canadian multiculturalism but a constitutive feature of it: the framework's celebration of difference paradoxically produces ontological exclusion of Black males through child welfare, education, employment, and criminal justice — requiring not institutional reform but philosophical rupture.

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper applies RSMT (Tommy Curry's framework developed in the US context) to Canadian multiculturalism for the first time. RSMT asserts that racialized maleness produces a 'phantom' category — Black manhood as phantasm constructed by societal imagination. Applied to Canada, RSMT reveals how multiculturalism's language of inclusion produces ontological exclusion of Black males through four institutional sectors. The paper critiques liberal minority rights frameworks (Kymlicka) as unable to account for this paradox.

epistemic_mode.qualitative_methods: Critical interpretive methodology combining philosophical frameworks (RSMT, Africana philosophy, critical race theory) with institutional analysis across four sectors: child welfare (removal statistics, 'unruly Black male child phantom'), education (streaming/tracking data, criminalization), criminal justice (over-representation in policing and incarceration), employment (economic exclusion). Uses qualitative sociology, policy analysis, and critical race studies literature.
data_sources: UN Working Group 2017 report on African Canadians
employment discrimination research on Black Canadian males
Africana philosophy / Black Male Studies secondary literature
epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Application of RSMT phantom categories to Canadian institutional sectors; critical reading of Canadian Multiculturalism Act and official discourse; genealogical connection between Ottoman/colonial practices and contemporary Canadian multiculturalism.
epistemic_mode.notes: The empirical evidence (institutional data across four sectors) is constitutive: the RSMT application cannot be made without it. The paper is normative_with_qualitative_empirical with constitutive evidence role. The theoretical contribution is the first systematic application of RSMT to the Canadian context — methodological innovation by crossing US-developed framework with Canadian multicultural ideology. Normative conclusion: philosophical rupture, not reform.

case_type.case_name: Black male disposability in Canadian multiculturalism — four institutional sectors

normative_stake.domain: race / multiculturalism / political philosophy / Africana philosophy

confidence_notes: Normative_with_qualitative_empirical with constitutive evidence role. The four-sector institutional analysis (child welfare, education, criminal justice, employment) is the backbone of the argument. The paper's move_sequence begins empirically (UN report) but the framing is primarily philosophical. This is one of the most empirically anchored papers in the PSC corpus after miyake_2021, tirkkonen_tietjen_2025, and fourie_2022. The normative conclusion (philosophical rupture, not reform) is strong and programmatic. The RSMT framework is an interpretive lens applied to qualitative-institutional evidence.

title: Whistleblowing as Civil Disobedience: The Case of Edward Snowden
author: Scheuerman

opening_move.description: Opening by diagnosing that media coverage of the Snowden NSA revelations has obscured the philosophically and politically serious question: whether Snowden's actions constitute legitimate civil disobedience. The paper begins with the gap between media spectacle and the underlying question of political morality.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Snowden's actions meet most demanding tests of civil disobedience; furthermore, his case advances liberal-democratic thinking on civil disobedience in two respects: questioning the punishment-acceptance requirement and updating civil disobedience theory for an era of globalization.

rivals_engaged: critics who deny Snowden's act qualifies as civil disobedience
Rawls's liberal account of civil disobedience
theoretical_positioning.description: The paper deploys the Snowden case to both defend the civil disobedience classification and advance civil disobedience theory. Two theoretical moves: (1) immanent critique of the orthodox punishment-acceptance requirement, using Snowden's own reasoning and the nature of surveillance state power to argue acceptance of punishment should not be preconditioned; (2) normative extension of civil disobedience theory for globalization contexts, where state surveillance crosses national boundaries.

epistemic_mode.qualitative_methods: Close analysis of Snowden's public statements, especially his 12 July 2013 Moscow airport speech; Snowden's Guardian interviews; Obama administration's response; Espionage Act proceedings. Reading Snowden's own arguments as philosophical contributions to civil disobedience theory.
data_sources: Snowden's public declarations (Guardian interviews, Moscow airport statement)
epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Political philosophy applied to contemporary case: reads Snowden's public reasoning as a contribution to civil disobedience theory, then evaluates whether his actions meet classical criteria (public, nonviolent, conscientious, political, law-breaking). Uses Rawls, Habermas, Thoreau, Gandhi, King as evaluative framework.
epistemic_mode.notes: The Snowden case functions as an interpretive case for political philosophy: it is read philosophically to adjudicate questions in civil disobedience theory. The paper is normative_with_interpretive_case because (1) Snowden's statements are read as philosophical arguments, not as empirical evidence; (2) the case is generative — it advances civil disobedience theory rather than merely illustrating it. Compare to firinci_orman_2025 (Arendt + youth climate activism) and scheuerman_2014's case is more philosophical than empirical.

case_type.case_name: Edward Snowden's NSA revelations (2013) — public statements and political reasoning

normative_stake.domain: civil disobedience / political philosophy / rule of law / globalization

confidence_notes: Classified normative_with_interpretive_case because Snowden's case is read philosophically (his statements as arguments) and is generative — the case drives theoretical revisions to civil disobedience theory. The classification sits between normative_with_interpretive_case and normative_with_qualitative_empirical: Snowden's statements are real-world documents but are read as philosophical texts rather than empirical evidence. The generative function of the case (advancing theory rather than illustrating pre-established positions) tips the classification toward normative_with_interpretive_case. The paper is from 2014 — the earliest paper in the PSC corpus, written closer to the Snowden events.

title: Vergangenheitsbewältigung Revisited: Distinguishing Two Paradigms of Working Through the Past
author: Schulz

opening_move.description: Opening from the 2020 racial justice protests and BLM movement's calls for 'working through' collective pasts, the paper revisits Thomas McCarthy's influential call for American Vergangenheitsbewältigung modelled on the German experience. Schulz diagnoses a conflation in McCarthy (following Habermas) of two distinct and often conflicting understandings of what 'working through' means — an idealist paradigm and a materialist paradigm.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Effective processes of working through the past require both idealist myth-building AND materialist deconstruction; but these must first be understood as distinct and in many ways conflicting before they can be brought together. The materialist paradigm (Adorno) acts as a corrective to idealist approaches that risk serving as reconciliation with ongoing structural injustice.

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper performs immanent critique of Habermas: his own mentor Adorno held a materially different conception of Aufarbeitung that Habermas conflates with his own idealist account. Schulz disentangles two paradigms: (1) idealist (Habermas/McCarthy): self-enlightenment through acknowledgment of past failures, collective re-narration, identity re-formation; (2) materialist (Adorno): uncovering structural endurance of past catastrophe, rejecting reconciliation, preventing future catastrophe through structural change. The conclusion synthesizes: effective working-through requires both, but their conflict must be acknowledged.

data_sources: Adorno's essays on working through the past
epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Careful reading of Adorno's and Habermas's texts on Vergangenheitsbewältigung; intellectual history of the concept; conceptual distinction building; normative synthesis in conclusion.
epistemic_mode.notes: The German Historikerstreit and BLM movement appear as motivating contexts, not as empirical cases under analysis. The paper's moves are philosophical: reading and distinguishing two paradigms in a theoretical tradition, then synthesizing them. Classified fully_normative. This is a Type 2 paper with a strong conceptual-distinction architecture: the entire argument turns on precisely distinguishing idealist from materialist paradigms.

normative_stake.domain: memory politics / political philosophy / Frankfurt School / racial justice

confidence_notes: This paper is a paradigmatic Type 2 (polemical reconstruction with conceptual distinction). The core argumentative move is deceptively simple: showing that Habermas misappropriates Adorno's conception of Aufarbeitung and conflates two distinct traditions. The normative conclusion is both the distinction itself and the synthesis (both paradigms are needed, but cannot be collapsed). The BLM/2020 protests are invoked only in the opening as contemporary relevance — the bulk of the argument is intellectual-historical exegesis of Adorno, Habermas, and McCarthy. High confidence.

title: If Nihilism is Murder, What Then? Camus' Distinctive Conception of Nihilism and Its Overcoming
author: Sharpe

opening_move.description: Opens by diagnosing Camus's persistent mischaracterization as a nihilist (due to Meursault, The Myth of Sisyphus's opening question about suicide). The paper argues this misreading misses how Camus from the start positioned nihilism as a problem to be overcome, not a condition to be embraced. The occasion is the renewed scholarly interest in nihilism (Jon Stewart's 2024 History of Nihilism) and the contemporary cultural-political climate.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Camus is a distinctive and undervalued thinker of nihilism whose post-1944 conception — nihilism as contempt for human life licensing deceit, enslavement, and murder — is a unique post-Nietzschean contribution irreducible to epistemological, reactionary, theological, or Nietzschean nihilism.

rivals_engaged: standard existentialist reading of Camus as nihilist
Novello's alignment of Camus with Nietzsche
four preceding conceptions of nihilism (epistemological, reactionary, theological, Nietzschean)
theoretical_positioning.description: The paper reconstructs Camus's distinctive nihilism concept in four parts: (1) maps four prior uses of 'nihilism' (Jacobi's epistemological, Russian-political, Christian-theological, Nietzschean); (2) reconstructs Camus's account of the absurd in early works, showing proximity and distance from prior nihilisms; (3) shows Camus's post-1944 definition (nihilism as contempt for human life licensing murder) as a new, historically-conditioned concept; (4) critiques Novello's alignment of Camus with Nietzsche, arguing Camus's 'measure' against Nietzsche's 'abyss' is a fundamental difference.

data_sources: Novello's Albert Camus as a Political Thinker (2010)
epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Intellectual history: genealogy of the term 'nihilism' from Jacobi through Nietzsche to 20th century; close reading of Camus's texts for his distinctive conception; comparative analysis between Camus's and Nietzsche's accounts; immanent critique of Novello's interpretation.
epistemic_mode.notes: This paper is purely_interpretive_hermeneutic because it makes no explicit normative claim about what follows for the present. The paper reconstructs Camus's historical-philosophical contribution to the nihilism debate and critiques a secondary reader. The contemporary relevance is gestured at in the opening (contemporary anomie, need for Camus's contribution) but not developed as a normative conclusion. The paper is closer to toribio_vazquez_2020 (genealogy of nihilism) and kastrinakis_2024 (Adorno-Nietzsche textual analysis) than to sharpe's potential alternative classification as normative.

normative_stake.domain: philosophy / nihilism / Camus / Nietzsche / critical theory of violence

key_concepts: nihilism as contempt for human life

engaged_critically: Freudian readings of Camus as nihilist

confidence_notes: Classified purely_interpretive_hermeneutic because the argument is entirely textual-genealogical: reconstructing Camus's distinctive conception of nihilism through close reading and intellectual history. No normative conclusion about what the present situation requires. The 'distinctive contribution' claim is descriptive-historical, not prescriptive. The contemporary climate reference in the opening is a motivation for the inquiry, not a normative application. Compare to toribio_vazquez_2020 (genealogy of nihilism) which has the same purely_interpretive_hermeneutic classification.

title: False Consciousness, Hermeneutical Injustice, and Ideological Power
author: Stahl

opening_move.description: Opens from the classic puzzle of critical social theory: why do members of oppressed groups frequently accept norms and support institutions that undermine their own interests? The paper diagnoses that two competing theoretical frameworks — Marxist ideology theory (false consciousness) and epistemic injustice theory (hermeneutical injustice, Fricker) — both attempt to explain this but each has serious deficiencies. The paper claims a third, undertheorized position in the Marxist tradition can synthesize the insights of both.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: A materialist ideology theory that locates epistemic constraint in 'distortions of concepts internal to social practices' (rather than in false beliefs or gaps in hermeneutical resources) avoids the problems of false consciousness accounts AND hermeneutical injustice accounts, providing a superior explanation of voluntary servitude.

rivals_engaged: false consciousness model of ideology (traditional Marxism)
theoretical_positioning.description: The paper constructs a four-section dialectic: (1) false consciousness theory and hermeneutical injustice theory are competitors because they share the same explananda; (2) false consciousness faces objections that make it implausible (paternalism objection, implausible epistemic mechanism); (3) hermeneutical injustice avoids these but lacks a plausible account of how social power distorts hermeneutical resources; (4) an alternative Marxist model (concepts distorted by their implication in dominating social practices) resolves both deficiencies. The synthesis draws on Lukács, Haslanger, and Celikates.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Analytical social epistemology: precise conceptual analysis of ideology, false consciousness, hermeneutical injustice; immanent critique of each model through internal objections; synthesis via 'distorted concepts internal to social practices' argument.
epistemic_mode.notes: Classic analytic political philosophy approach: argument through precise conceptual distinctions, internal objections to competing models, synthetic conclusion. No empirical evidence; no case. The paper is a Type 2a paper within a social epistemology subdomain — bridging Marxist ideology theory and analytic epistemic injustice literature. Similar in structure to bufkin_2024 (which does something analogous for racism specifically) and stahl is noted as extending that work.

normative_stake.domain: social epistemology / ideology theory / critical theory / epistemic injustice

confidence_notes: Paradigmatic Type 2a (polemical reconstruction via analytic method) in the social epistemology subdomain. The paper's four-step dialectic is tightly constructed and fully within conceptual-normative space. The paper explicitly notes its relation to bufkin_2024 (which covers similar terrain for the racism-specific case). High confidence in fully_normative classification.

title: Why (and How) Statues Matter
author: Stopford

opening_move.description: Opening from Rhodes Must Fall and BLM decolonial activism around statues, the paper diagnoses that popular and academic debates rest on an unexamined metaphysical presupposition: statues as singular, discrete objects innocent of the histories attached to them. This presupposition, Stopford argues, truncates the horizon of decolonial political possibility. The paper's intervention: mobilize analytic metaphysics (material constitution debate) to show statues are multiple, co-locating objects materially bound to ongoing post-colonial relations.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: A metaphysics of statues as co-constituting, relational objects (following Heil's material constitution analysis) provides philosophical grounding for why decolonial activists consider statues crucial — statues are not merely symbolic but are nodal points in the latticework of ongoing colonial power relations.

rivals_engaged: anti-decolonial metaphysics (statues as singular, innocent hunks of matter)
liberal conception (statues as symbolic proxies of a past they are innocent of)
heritage discourse (statues as heritage objects cut adrift from historical context)
theoretical_positioning.description: The paper constructs an analytic metaphysics of statues by applying John Heil's material constitution analysis: statues are multiple, co-locating objects whose identity conditions include other objects and social practices. Applied to decolonial activism, this means statues are materially constituted through post-colonial power relations — they are 'plenipotentiaries of hegemonic psychical and libidinal energy' (Rao). The paper derives decolonial implications: removing a statue does not erase history but dissolves a node of colonial power.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Analytic metaphysics: engages material constitution debate (Heil, Wiggins, Doepke) on identity of material objects; applies this to social ontology of statues; reads decolonial activism through this metaphysical lens.
epistemic_mode.notes: This paper is an unusual entry in the PSC corpus: it uses analytic metaphysics (material constitution) to ground decolonial political theory. The argument is fully normative — the metaphysical analysis supports a normative conclusion about the politics of statues. No empirical evidence; the decolonial cases (Cecil Rhodes statue, Colston statue in Bristol) appear as illustrative examples in the philosophical argument. Classified fully_normative with a unique methodological profile: continental-political ends, analytic-metaphysical means.

normative_stake.domain: decolonial theory / metaphysics / political ontology / memory politics

confidence_notes: A methodologically distinctive paper: it is the only paper in the PSC corpus that grounds a decolonial normative argument in analytic metaphysics. Classified fully_normative because the argument moves in conceptual-normative space throughout: the 'statues as multiple co-locating objects' thesis is a philosophical claim used to derive normative conclusions about decolonial activism. The case examples (Colston, Rhodes) are philosophical illustrations, not empirical cases under study. The paper has an unusual move_sequence: puzzle_diagnosis → rival_mapping (metaphysical) → framework_proposal (Heil's material constitution) → case_introduction (colonial object concept) → case_development (application to decolonial activism) → normative_derivation.

title: Loneliness and Radicalization
author: Tirkkonen and Tietjen

opening_move.description: Opening from Arendt's analysis of loneliness as a breeding ground for totalitarianism (Origins of Totalitarianism), the paper diagnoses that the political dimension of loneliness has not been incorporated into empirical research on contemporary radicalization, extremism, and terrorism. There is a gap between theoretical debates on loneliness and empirical research on radicalization that this paper bridges.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: An empirically grounded theory of loneliness in contemporary radicalization reveals: for right-wing lone-actor terrorists, fear of individual and collective extinction is central; for Islamist radicalization in Western countries, experiences of discrimination and marginalization are crucial. These two modes require different theoretical and political responses.

rivals_engaged: Arendtian political loneliness theory (extending and correcting)
theoretical_positioning.description: The paper extends Arendt's political loneliness theory by combining it with a critical phenomenological approach that takes subjective experience seriously while situating it in structural social conditions. It applies this combined framework to two case sets: lone-actor right-wing terrorists and female Western ISIS affiliates. The extension of Arendt involves incorporating social exclusion, discrimination, and marginalization as structural conditions shaping the phenomenology of loneliness.

epistemic_mode.qualitative_methods: Case study analysis of two sets: (1) lone-actor right-wing terrorists — analysis of manifestos, public statements, investigative psychological assessments of Breivik, Tarrant, and similar cases; (2) female Western ISIS affiliates — qualitative research on recruitment narratives, testimonies, and secondary literature on ISIS foreign fighters. Critical phenomenological reading of case materials.
data_sources: secondary empirical literature on radicalization and terrorism
case study materials on lone-actor terrorists (Breivik, Tarrant)
qualitative research on female ISIS affiliates from Western countries
epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Critical phenomenology: combines first-person phenomenological analysis of loneliness with structural-social situatedness. Case analysis guided by Arendtian conceptual framework. Comparative analysis of two types of radicalization.
epistemic_mode.notes: This is an empirically grounded theory paper — closest to the Type 5/6 cluster (empirically anchored normative theory). The empirical evidence is constitutive: the argument about differentiated loneliness types (extinction fear vs. marginalization) requires and is derived from the case analysis. The paper is normative_with_qualitative_empirical with constitutive evidence role.

case_type.case_name: Two sets: lone-actor right-wing terrorists; female Western ISIS affiliates

normative_stake.domain: political theory / radicalization / loneliness / terrorism / democracy

confidence_notes: This is one of the most empirically anchored papers in the PSC corpus. The normative-theoretical framing (Arendt, critical phenomenology) is genuine, but the paper bridges philosophy and empirical social science more explicitly than most others. The move_sequence follows the framework-first, case-second pattern (like firinci_orman_2025 and tirkkonen/tietjen is more empirically engaged). The two-case comparative structure (lone-actor vs. ISIS women) is distinctive. Classified normative_with_qualitative_empirical with constitutive evidence role.

title: Nietzsche's Shadow: On the Origin and Development of the Term Nihilism
author: Toribio Vazquez

opening_move.description: Opens from the paradox of nihilism: a term that is both widely used and fundamentally contested in meaning. Despite Nietzsche's role as the iconic 'philosopher of nihilism,' posthumous uses of the word deviate sharply from his own determinations — and Nietzsche's reputation itself misunderstands what he meant by nihilism. The paper offers a genealogy to clarify the term's prevalence and ambiguity.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: The term 'nihilism' entered philosophy through Jacobi's critique of Fichte, was popularized by Russian political nihilism, and was reconstructed by Nietzsche in a sense that posthumous users largely abandoned — resulting in systematic conceptual confusion that the genealogy aims to clarify.

rivals_engaged: popular and academic conflations of nihilism
Nietzsche scholarship that misreads his conception
reactionary/political uses of 'nihilism' as a slur
theoretical_positioning.description: The paper traces the genealogy of 'nihilism' through three phases: (1) pre-Nietzschean uses (Jacobi, Stirner, Russian political nihilism); (2) Nietzsche's early conception (revaluation of values, nihilism as diagnosis of European civilization, not celebration); (3) 20th century posthumous uses that deviate from Nietzsche's own meaning. The paper shows that Nietzsche is the 'founder of discursivity' on nihilism but also the figure whose specific meaning is most systematically misunderstood.

data_sources: Nietzsche's early works (The Gay Science, Thus Spoke Zarathustra, The Will to Power fragments)
20th century uses in philosophy, politics, and mass culture
epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Conceptual genealogy: traces a term's history through multiple contexts and usages; identifies semantic shifts; reconstructs the 'authentic' Nietzschean usage against which deviations are measured.
epistemic_mode.notes: Purely_interpretive_hermeneutic: the paper is a conceptual genealogy making no prescriptive claims. Its normative implication — that we should understand nihilism more carefully — is implicit but not stated as a normative conclusion. Closely related to sharpe_2025 (which reconstructs Camus's distinctive conception against the same confusion) and kastrinakis_2024 (Adorno-Nietzsche textual genealogy).

normative_stake.domain: philosophy / intellectual history / nihilism / Nietzsche

confidence_notes: Shortest and most compressed of the PSC hermeneutic papers. Purely genealogical-descriptive: traces term history without normative application. The normative_stake is low because the paper does not claim implications for present political theory or practice. The move_sequence is minimal. Compare to sharpe_2025 (similar genealogical reconstruction but more elaborate and with higher normative stake — Camus is positioned as a neglected resource for the present). High confidence in purely_interpretive_hermeneutic classification.

title: The Theory of the Public Sphere as a Cognitive Theory of Modern Society
author: Trenz

opening_move.description: Opening by noting that Habermas's public sphere theory (Structural Transformation, 1962) is usually discussed as normative political theory, with contested applicability. Trenz diagnoses an underexplored 'second reading': the theory as a sociological-cognitive account of how modern society creates shared knowledge and drives social change through critical self-reflection. The paper makes this second reading explicit and shows its contemporary relevance.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: The theory of the public sphere, read as a cognitive theory of modern society, shows that public sphere principles are simultaneously undermined AND gain new relevance in the context of digitalization and globalization — social learning processes through publicity remain possible even as their conditions transform.

rivals_engaged: institutional/deliberative approaches that ignore cognitive dimension
theoretical_positioning.description: The paper reconstructs Habermas's public sphere theory from its cognitive dimension — how publicity creates shared social visibility, enables critical self-reflection, and drives social change. This reading integrates the critical-Marxist (commodification) and emancipatory-institutionalist strands of Habermas interpretation as 'mutually dependent.' The paper then extends this cognitive reading to contemporary digital transformation, arguing that the same principles are both threatened and renewed.

data_sources: Habermas's works (Structural Transformation, Between Facts and Norms, subsequent essays)
epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Intellectual history and theoretical reconstruction: traces the history of the public sphere concept from Habermas's early work through later developments; reconstructs the cognitive-sociological dimension; extends to digital era through normative argument.
epistemic_mode.notes: References to contemporary digital media (social media algorithms, platform economy) are made at a high level of abstraction as theoretical-normative claims, not as empirical analysis. This distinguishes Trenz from nosthoff_maschewski_2023 and fischer_jarren_2023, which ground their arguments in specific empirical material. Trenz remains at the theoretical-reconstructive level. Classified fully_normative.

normative_stake.domain: public sphere theory / democratic theory / digitalization / social epistemology

key_concepts: cognitive theory of the public sphere
confidence_notes: A theoretical companion to the other public sphere papers in the PSC 2024 Vol.50(1) special issue (brunkhorst, fischer_jarren, nosthoff_maschewski, kempf_2023, kempf_sevignani). Trenz operates at the highest level of abstraction — reconstructing the theoretical architecture of Habermas's public sphere theory rather than applying it to empirical cases. Classified fully_normative because the contemporary extension (digitalization) is handled through normative theoretical argument, not empirical analysis. Compare to kellner_2024 (another fully normative Habermas reconstruction in PSC).

title: A Call for Psycho-Affective Change: Fanon, Feminism, and White Negrophobic Femininity
author: Yokum

opening_move.description: Opens by diagnosing that Susan Brownmiller's virulent critique of Fanon in Against Our Will — charging him with misogyny, personal anguish, and confusion — has remained unanswered and standing, despite being widely cited. The puzzle: is Brownmiller's charge that Fanon hates women and obsesses over rape justified, or does it misread Fanon's sociogenic and psychoanalytic project? The paper defends Fanon against this charge.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Fanon's analysis of white negrophobic femininity is best interpreted not as personal misogyny or justification of rape but as an acerbic critique of colonial society that produces such psycho-affective disorders — ultimately calling for psycho-affective change and liberation for both colonized Blacks and colonizing whites.

rivals_engaged: Brownmiller's reading of Fanon as misogynist
feminist critics who see Fanon as antifeminist (hooks, Bergner, Kalisa)
decontextualized readings of Fanon's rape fantasy passages
theoretical_positioning.description: The paper performs immanent critique of Brownmiller: argues her charge misreads Fanon by ignoring (1) his sociogenic project (not merely psychoanalytic — the colonial situation is the pathological agent); (2) the critical-theoretical function of his discussion of negrophobia (diagnose to destroy); (3) the distinction between reporting/analyzing rape fantasies and condoning rape. The reconstruction of Fanon shows his account of white negrophobic femininity as a critique of colonial society, not an attack on white women.

data_sources: Fanon's The Wretched of the Earth
epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Philosophical hermeneutics: careful contextual reading of disputed passages in Fanon; reconstruction of his psychoanalytic and sociogenic method; immanent critique of Brownmiller through close reading; comparison with how Sharpley-Whiting and Rabaka read the same material.
epistemic_mode.notes: This is primarily a hermeneutic-rehabilitative paper: its primary goal is to correctly read Fanon, not to derive a normative political conclusion for the present. The 'call for psycho-affective change' in the title is Fanon's normative claim, not Yokum's independent normative thesis. Classified purely_interpretive_hermeneutic. Compare to engelman_2023 (Feuerbach-Marx senses) and kastrinakis_2024 (Adorno-Nietzsche): all three are interpretive rehabilitations without independent normative conclusion. Distinct from dyson_2024 (which reconstructs Fanon to derive an independent normative argument about institutional psychotherapy).

normative_stake.domain: Fanon studies / feminist philosophy / postcolonial theory / race

confidence_notes: The classification purely_interpretive_hermeneutic rests on the paper's primary function: defending a correct reading of Fanon's Black Skin, White Masks against misreading. The paper does not derive an independent normative claim about what should be done in the present; the 'call for psycho-affective change' is Fanon's own conclusion, which Yokum reconstructs and defends. High confidence.

title: From Empire to Nation: Management of Religious Pluralism in the Ottoman Empire and Turkey
author: Çevik

opening_move.description: Opens from the puzzle of post-Cold War nostalgia for empires as managers of pluralism: as nation-states have failed to manage ethnic and religious diversity, scholars have revisited empires — especially the Ottoman Empire — as models of tolerance. The paper diagnoses a specific historical puzzle: how did the Ottoman Empire, traditionally more pluralistic than Turkey, transition to a less tolerant secular republic?
opening_move.stakes_claimed: The Ottoman modernization reforms of the 19th century transformed the empire into a more nation-state-like structure, making religious pluralism harder to sustain and producing the homogenizing nationalism that led to the exclusion of religious minorities and was inherited by the Turkish Republic — which became less tolerant than the Islamic empire it replaced.

rivals_engaged: optimistic post-Cold War empire literature (Barkey, Maier)
liberal multiculturalism as model for post-Ottoman states
theoretical_positioning.description: The paper provides a historical-analytical argument about the Ottoman/Turkish transition, framed theoretically through the empire vs. nation-state contrast (indirect rule, plurality, autonomy vs. direct rule, uniformity, homogenization). It traces the millet system's evolution from pluralistic autonomy to contested homogenizing pressure, arguing that modernization (centralization, Tanzimat reforms) made the empire more nation-state-like and less tolerant, a process completed by the Republic.

epistemic_mode.qualitative_methods: Historical analysis: examination of Ottoman millet system from 19th century through Republic of Turkey; Tanzimat reform period; treatment of religious minorities (Greeks, Armenians, Jews) across historical periods; analysis of political decision-making in Ottoman and Republican contexts. Uses comparative empire studies methodology.
data_sources: Ottoman historical sources and secondary Ottoman historiography
Barkey, Lieven, Maier, Kumar on empire comparative literature
epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Comparative historical analysis: uses conceptual frameworks from empire studies (Eisenstadt, Doyle, Barkey) to analyze Ottoman/Turkish historical trajectory. Draws conclusions about the structural relationship between modernization, nationalism, and minority exclusion.
epistemic_mode.notes: This paper is primarily_empirical_qualitative: the argument is primarily historical-comparative, with the normative implications (about tolerance and pluralism) derived from the historical analysis. The normative stake is present (the conclusion implies lessons about empire/nation-state and pluralism management) but is secondary to the historical argument. This is the most historically empirical paper in the PSC corpus alongside fourie_2022 and miyake_2021. The short length (about 11 pages) suggests a conference proceedings format.

case_type.case_name: Ottoman Empire to Turkish Republic transition — millet system and religious pluralism

normative_stake.domain: empire studies / religious pluralism / Ottoman/Turkish history / nationalism

confidence_notes: Classified primarily_empirical_qualitative because the paper's primary mode is historical-comparative analysis, with normative conclusions following from historical findings. The normative stake is present but medium: the paper implies lessons about pluralism management but doesn't develop a prescriptive political theory. This distinguishes it from fully_normative papers (where the historical material is used as philosophical resource) and from normative_with_qualitative_empirical papers (where empirical evidence supports a prior normative framework). Here the historical analysis is primary. Conference proceedings origin (Dublin Seminars 2023) explains the shorter length and less elaborate theoretical framing.
